paragraphs: Geysers are hot springs that periodically erupt, ejecting tall columns of heated water and steam high into the air. Early naturalists in the eighteenth century regarded these fountains merely as curiosities, offering no mechanism for their rhythmic behavior. [■] They assumed that a hidden fire directly boiled the water at the surface, an idea that explained the visible heat yet completely ignored the striking regularity with which the bursts recurred.

This surface-bound guessing gave way during the nineteenth century to a more physical account of the underground plumbing. [■] Investigators proposed that groundwater slowly collects in a narrow subterranean channel, where the weight of the overlying column raises the boiling point far above the normal value. When the deepest water finally flashes into steam, the sudden expansion drives the eruption upward. [■] This model was incomplete, however, because it pictured every geyser as one simple vertical tube.

Modern researchers, equipped with downhole cameras and buried temperature sensors, have substantially revised that tidy picture. [■] They now find that many geysers depend on tangled networks of side chambers that trap pockets of steam and quietly govern the timing of each eruption. Researchers are continuously uncovering how these hidden cavities store and release energy, steadily refining their predictions of when a given geyser will next erupt.
items.passage:
Geysers are hot springs that periodically erupt, ejecting tall columns of heated water and steam high into the air. Early naturalists in the eighteenth century regarded these fountains merely as curiosities, offering no mechanism for their rhythmic behavior. [■] They assumed that a hidden fire directly boiled the water at the surface, an idea that explained the visible heat yet completely ignored the striking regularity with which the bursts recurred.

This surface-bound guessing gave way during the nineteenth century to a more physical account of the underground plumbing. [■] Investigators proposed that groundwater slowly collects in a narrow subterranean channel, where the weight of the overlying column raises the boiling point far above the normal value. When the deepest water finally flashes into steam, the sudden expansion drives the eruption upward. [■] This model was incomplete, however, because it pictured every geyser as one simple vertical tube.

Modern researchers, equipped with downhole cameras and buried temperature sensors, have substantially revised that tidy picture. [■] They now find that many geysers depend on tangled networks of side chambers that trap pockets of steam and quietly govern the timing of each eruption. Researchers are continuously uncovering how these hidden cavities store and release energy, steadily refining their predictions of when a given geyser will next erupt.

questions.stem: The word "govern" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: predict
options.B: control
options.C: delay
options.D: record

questions.explanation: The passage says the side chambers "quietly govern the timing of each eruption," meaning they control that timing; "control" is the fitting synonym.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what causes a geyser to erupt in the nineteenth-century model?

options.A: A hidden surface fire boils the water directly
options.B: Cold rainwater mixes with rising volcanic gas
options.C: Earthquakes shake water loose from the channel
options.D: Water in the channel abruptly flashes to steam

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "when the deepest water finally flashes into steam, the sudden expansion drives the eruption upward," matching option D.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about geyser eruptions?

options.A: Their timing depends on more than one channel
options.B: They occur at entirely random, unpredictable moments
options.C: Downhole cameras were first designed for volcano research
options.D: Every geyser shares one identical underground structure

questions.explanation: Because paragraph 3 says many geysers rely on "tangled networks of side chambers" that govern timing, the eruption schedule must depend on more than a lone vertical channel.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention "downhole cameras and buried temperature sensors" in paragraph 3?

options.A: To argue that earlier investigators had been careless
options.B: To suggest that direct observation has proven unhelpful
options.C: To explain how researchers gathered evidence to revise it
options.D: To catalog the rising expense of studying one geyser

questions.explanation: The instruments are cited as the means by which "modern researchers... have substantially revised that tidy picture," so they explain how new evidence was gathered.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'Lacking any means to observe the plumbing hidden below ground, these early observers could only speculate from what erupted at the surface.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1 (the first ■)
options.B: Location 2 (the second ■)
options.C: Location 3 (the third ■)
options.D: Location 4 (the fourth ■)

questions.explanation: The sentence explains why the eighteenth-century "early observers" could offer no mechanism, so it fits at Location 1, right after they are said to give "no mechanism for their rhythmic behavior" and before "They assumed that a hidden fire..."

paragraphs: Magnetism is a fundamental force that arises from the motion of electric charge, most familiar in the invisible attraction between certain metals. Every electron behaves as a minuscule magnet because it carries both electric charge and an intrinsic property called spin. [■] In most ordinary materials these atomic magnets point in random directions, so their separate fields cancel one another and no overall magnetism can be detected.

This cancellation breaks down in a small group of metals such as iron, nickel, and cobalt. [■] Within these ferromagnetic metals, neighboring atoms lock their spins into the same orientation, forming microscopic regions called domains in which countless electrons align. When an external field nudges these domains to grow and point together, the metal becomes strongly magnetized. [■] Removing the field can leave much of that alignment intact, producing a permanent magnet.

The strength of any magnet therefore depends on how completely its domains can be aligned and then held in place. [■] Engineers exploit this fact by blending elements that resist the random thermal jostling which would otherwise scramble the alignment. Designers now apply these principles when building compact electric motors, dense magnetic data storage, and the powerful coils inside hospital imaging scanners.
items.passage:
Magnetism is a fundamental force that arises from the motion of electric charge, most familiar in the invisible attraction between certain metals. Every electron behaves as a minuscule magnet because it carries both electric charge and an intrinsic property called spin. [■] In most ordinary materials these atomic magnets point in random directions, so their separate fields cancel one another and no overall magnetism can be detected.

This cancellation breaks down in a small group of metals such as iron, nickel, and cobalt. [■] Within these ferromagnetic metals, neighboring atoms lock their spins into the same orientation, forming microscopic regions called domains in which countless electrons align. When an external field nudges these domains to grow and point together, the metal becomes strongly magnetized. [■] Removing the field can leave much of that alignment intact, producing a permanent magnet.

The strength of any magnet therefore depends on how completely its domains can be aligned and then held in place. [■] Engineers exploit this fact by blending elements that resist the random thermal jostling which would otherwise scramble the alignment. Designers now apply these principles when building compact electric motors, dense magnetic data storage, and the powerful coils inside hospital imaging scanners.

questions.stem: The word "intrinsic" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: external
options.B: magnetic
options.C: inherent
options.D: temporary

questions.explanation: An "intrinsic property called spin" is one the electron possesses in itself, so "inherent" is the closest synonym.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what forms a magnetic domain?

options.A: Neighboring atoms whose spins point the same way
options.B: Electrons that have lost their charge and spin
options.C: Layers of metal heated until they melt together
options.D: Atoms arranged into a regular crystal lattice

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "neighboring atoms lock their spins into the same orientation, forming microscopic regions called domains," matching option A.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT:

options.A: Electrons act as tiny magnets because of their spin
options.B: A magnet's strength is unrelated to domain alignment
options.C: In most materials, atomic magnetic fields cancel out
options.D: Iron, nickel, and cobalt can become strongly magnetized

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says a magnet's strength "depends on how completely its domains can be aligned," so B contradicts the passage; A, C, and D are all stated.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about a magnet exposed to strong heat?

options.A: Heating a magnet reliably increases its magnetic strength
options.B: Larger magnets are always more expensive to manufacture
options.C: Only medical scanners can make use of permanent magnets
options.D: Excessive heat could disrupt a magnet's alignment

questions.explanation: Since engineers must choose elements that "resist the random thermal jostling which would otherwise scramble the alignment," enough heat would disrupt that alignment and weaken the magnet.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, an ordinary iron nail, though not magnetic on its own, can be turned into a weak magnet by stroking it with an existing one.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1 (the first ■)
options.B: Location 2 (the second ■)
options.C: Location 3 (the third ■)
options.D: Location 4 (the fourth ■)

questions.explanation: The iron-nail example illustrates the claim that cancellation "breaks down" in metals such as iron, so it fits at Location 2, immediately after iron is named and before the domains of ferromagnetic metals are explained.

paragraphs: Impressionism was a nineteenth-century French painting movement that sought to capture the fleeting effects of light and atmosphere rather than precise, finished detail. Its painters abandoned the smooth surfaces and somber tones prized by the traditional academies. [■] Instead they worked rapidly outdoors, laying down broken strokes of bright, unmixed pigment that a viewer's eye would blend together at a distance.

This technique rested on a deliberate trust in the observer's own vision. [■] Rather than blending green on the palette, an Impressionist might place separate dabs of pure blue and yellow side by side, letting them fuse optically into a livelier hue. [■] Because sunlight shifts constantly through the day, these artists painted quickly, often finishing a canvas in a single session to preserve one exact moment of changing light.

Such radical methods reshaped how an entire generation of later painters understood color and perception, freeing them from the long-standing obligation to reproduce every visible contour. [■] The movement's influence spread rapidly well beyond France within just a few decades. However, some critics still argue that this narrow focus on momentary sensation left Impressionism unable to convey the deeper structure and enduring meaning that more deliberate, carefully planned compositions achieve.
items.passage:
Impressionism was a nineteenth-century French painting movement that sought to capture the fleeting effects of light and atmosphere rather than precise, finished detail. Its painters abandoned the smooth surfaces and somber tones prized by the traditional academies. [■] Instead they worked rapidly outdoors, laying down broken strokes of bright, unmixed pigment that a viewer's eye would blend together at a distance.

This technique rested on a deliberate trust in the observer's own vision. [■] Rather than blending green on the palette, an Impressionist might place separate dabs of pure blue and yellow side by side, letting them fuse optically into a livelier hue. [■] Because sunlight shifts constantly through the day, these artists painted quickly, often finishing a canvas in a single session to preserve one exact moment of changing light.

Such radical methods reshaped how an entire generation of later painters understood color and perception, freeing them from the long-standing obligation to reproduce every visible contour. [■] The movement's influence spread rapidly well beyond France within just a few decades. However, some critics still argue that this narrow focus on momentary sensation left Impressionism unable to convey the deeper structure and enduring meaning that more deliberate, carefully planned compositions achieve.

questions.stem: The word "somber" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: bright
options.B: gloomy
options.C: costly
options.D: faded

questions.explanation: The "somber tones" of the academies are contrasted with the Impressionists' "bright" pigment, so "somber" means dark or gloomy.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, why did Impressionists place unmixed colors side by side?

options.A: So the palette would stay clean between painting sessions
options.B: So the colors would fuse within the viewer's eye
options.C: So the wet paint would dry much faster outdoors
options.D: So the canvas would require fewer layers of pigment

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains that an Impressionist placed dabs of blue and yellow side by side, "letting them fuse optically into a livelier hue," i.e. blending in the viewer's eye.

questions.stem: What does "them" refer to in paragraph 2?

options.A: the academic painters
options.B: the observer's own eyes
options.C: the separate dabs of pigment
options.D: the shifting rays of sunlight

questions.explanation: The clause "letting them fuse optically" refers to the "separate dabs of pure blue and yellow" placed side by side.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about painters who came after the Impressionists?

options.A: They felt less bound to reproduce exact detail
options.B: They forced painting back toward strictly precise realism
options.C: Their canvases sold for record prices abroad
options.D: They all rejected Impressionist ideas completely

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says the methods reshaped later painters' understanding of color, "freeing them from the long-standing obligation to reproduce every visible contour," implying they felt less bound to exact detail.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, the young painters later known as Post-Impressionists borrowed this freedom to distort color for emotional effect.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1 (the first ■)
options.B: Location 2 (the second ■)
options.C: Location 3 (the third ■)
options.D: Location 4 (the fourth ■)

questions.explanation: The example of later painters using this new "freedom" elaborates the legacy point about "later painters," so it fits at Location 4, right after they are said to be freed from reproducing every contour and before the movement's wider influence is described.

paragraphs: A suspension bridge is a structure whose roadway hangs from steel cables draped between two tall towers. This design allows it to span far greater distances than a bridge supported by piers alone. The main cables carry the weight of the deck, transferring the load along their curved length toward the towers and the massive anchorages at each end.

These anchorages are the key to the whole system. Each main cable pulls inward with enormous tension, and only a heavy anchor block buried in rock or concrete can resist being dragged toward the center of the span. From the main cables hang slender vertical wires, called suspenders, that grip the deck at regular intervals and hold it level against the constant pull of gravity.

Wind poses the subtlest danger to such slender spans. A steady gust can set a lightweight deck oscillating, and if the rhythm matches the bridge's natural sway, the motion grows until the structure tears itself apart. Modern engineers therefore shape the deck like an aircraft wing and add internal trusses, letting air slip past smoothly so that the completed span holds steady in strong wind.
items.passage:
A suspension bridge is a structure whose roadway hangs from steel cables draped between two tall towers. This design allows it to span far greater distances than a bridge supported by piers alone. The main cables carry the weight of the deck, transferring the load along their curved length toward the towers and the massive anchorages at each end.

These anchorages are the key to the whole system. Each main cable pulls inward with enormous tension, and only a heavy anchor block buried in rock or concrete can resist being dragged toward the center of the span. From the main cables hang slender vertical wires, called suspenders, that grip the deck at regular intervals and hold it level against the constant pull of gravity.

Wind poses the subtlest danger to such slender spans. A steady gust can set a lightweight deck oscillating, and if the rhythm matches the bridge's natural sway, the motion grows until the structure tears itself apart. Modern engineers therefore shape the deck like an aircraft wing and add internal trusses, letting air slip past smoothly so that the completed span holds steady in strong wind.

questions.stem: The word "slender" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: heavy
options.B: thin
options.C: tall
options.D: curved

questions.explanation: The "slender vertical wires" are the thin suspenders hanging from the cables, so "slender" means thin.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what carries the weight of the bridge's deck?

options.A: The piers standing directly beneath the roadway
options.B: The steel trusses hidden inside the towers
options.C: The main cables draped between the towers
options.D: The concrete deck's own internal reinforcement

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that "the main cables carry the weight of the deck, transferring the load... toward the towers and the massive anchorages," matching option C.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about a bridge's anchorages?

options.A: The anchorages bear almost none of the cable's force
options.B: Anchorages are usually built after the deck is finished
options.C: A bridge would fail if its anchorages ever shifted
options.D: Every bridge type depends on buried anchor blocks

questions.explanation: Since only a heavy anchor block "can resist being dragged toward the center of the span," an anchorage that shifted would let the cables give way and the bridge collapse.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention "an aircraft wing" in paragraph 3?

options.A: To illustrate how a streamlined deck lets wind slip past
options.B: To argue that bridges and aircraft share designers
options.C: To show that decks should catch as much wind as possible
options.D: To describe the exact width of a modern roadway

questions.explanation: Engineers "shape the deck like an aircraft wing... letting air slip past smoothly," so the comparison illustrates how a streamlined deck sheds wind.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT:

options.A: The main cables transfer their load toward the towers
options.B: Anchor blocks resist the inward pull of the cables
options.C: Suspenders hold the deck level against gravity
options.D: The towers hang suspended from the roadway deck

questions.explanation: The passage says the roadway "hangs from steel cables draped between two tall towers," so the towers support the deck, not the reverse; A, B, and C are all stated.

paragraphs: A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without itself being consumed or permanently altered by it. Many industrially valuable reactions proceed far too slowly at ordinary room temperatures to be commercially useful. Raising the temperature can indeed accelerate them, but heating enormous volumes of raw material demands costly energy and can destroy delicate products, leaving manufacturers facing an expensive and wasteful dilemma.

Catalysts resolve this fundamental problem by offering the reacting molecules an easier, lower-energy chemical pathway. A catalyst binds the reactants to its surface, weakening their internal bonds so that they combine using far less energy than the direct route would require. Because the catalyst itself emerges unchanged, a small quantity can process an enormous stream of material, driving the reaction rapidly at gentle, economical temperatures.

These advantages have made catalysts central to modern chemistry, from refining crude fuels to manufacturing agricultural fertilizer. Yet real catalysts gradually lose their power as unwanted impurities coat their active surfaces, a process known as poisoning. Chemists are now designing more resilient materials that resist such contamination, and this steady progress promises even more efficient and sustainable industrial reactions.
items.passage:
A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without itself being consumed or permanently altered by it. Many industrially valuable reactions proceed far too slowly at ordinary room temperatures to be commercially useful. Raising the temperature can indeed accelerate them, but heating enormous volumes of raw material demands costly energy and can destroy delicate products, leaving manufacturers facing an expensive and wasteful dilemma.

Catalysts resolve this fundamental problem by offering the reacting molecules an easier, lower-energy chemical pathway. A catalyst binds the reactants to its surface, weakening their internal bonds so that they combine using far less energy than the direct route would require. Because the catalyst itself emerges unchanged, a small quantity can process an enormous stream of material, driving the reaction rapidly at gentle, economical temperatures.

These advantages have made catalysts central to modern chemistry, from refining crude fuels to manufacturing agricultural fertilizer. Yet real catalysts gradually lose their power as unwanted impurities coat their active surfaces, a process known as poisoning. Chemists are now designing more resilient materials that resist such contamination, and this steady progress promises even more efficient and sustainable industrial reactions.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: Why raising the temperature destroys delicate chemical products
options.B: How catalysts speed reactions and chemists improve them
options.C: How impurities gradually poison a catalyst's active surface
options.D: Why fertilizer production demands enormous energy input

questions.explanation: The passage defines catalysts, explains how they accelerate reactions cheaply, and describes efforts to make them last longer, so B captures its full scope; the others are single details.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what is one drawback of using heat to speed reactions?

options.A: It can ruin fragile products and waste costly energy
options.B: It permanently uses up the catalyst on its surface
options.C: It releases toxic gases into the surrounding air
options.D: It makes the final product much harder to purify

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says heating "demands costly energy and can destroy delicate products," matching option A.

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: It gives historical background for the claims in paragraph 1
options.B: It contradicts the main argument made in paragraph 1
options.C: It presents the solution to the problem in paragraph 1
options.D: It provides statistical evidence supporting paragraph 1

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 lays out the problem of slow, energy-hungry reactions, and paragraph 2 opens "Catalysts resolve this fundamental problem," so it presents the solution.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about a catalyst?

options.A: A fresh catalyst is required for each separate reaction
options.B: Catalysts work only on reactions that involve metals
options.C: The same catalyst can be reused many times over
options.D: Catalysts raise the temperature that a reaction needs

questions.explanation: Because "the catalyst itself emerges unchanged" and a small quantity can process an enormous stream of material, the same catalyst can serve many times over.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what causes a catalyst to lose its effectiveness?

options.A: Rising temperatures that slowly melt its surface
options.B: Repeated washing that dissolves the active metal
options.C: Long storage that dries out the material
options.D: Impurities that gradually coat its active surface

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 states that catalysts "gradually lose their power as unwanted impurities coat their active surfaces, a process known as poisoning," matching option D.
